nativeRange.habitat: The Compressiceps inhabits the rocky littoral zone of Lake Tanganyika, where boulder-strewn slopes, crevice-rich cliff faces, and rock rubble extend from shallow inshore areas down to deeper rocky drop-offs. The water throughout this zone is highly oxygenated, crystal-clear, hard, and strongly alkaline.

decorPreferences: large piles of rocks with narrow crevices and caves
empty snail shells (Neothauma tanganyicense or alternatives) for female spawning sites
sandy substrate between rock formations for shell-dweller colonies
minimal planting — hardy species such as Anubias or Vallisneria if desired
ample open rock face for foraging and territorial display

diet.notes: In the wild, the Compressiceps is a predator of small fish, fry, eggs, and invertebrates, using its laterally compressed profile to insert its head into rock crevices and shell openings where other fish cannot follow. In captivity, most specimens accept quality frozen foods — brine shrimp, mysis shrimp, bloodworms, and krill — along with small sinking cichlid pellets and carnivore sticks. Newly acquired fish may initially refuse prepared foods; offering live or frozen brine shrimp and mysis shrimp tends to trigger the feeding response before a gradual transition to pellets. The Compressiceps does not graze algae. Feed sparingly — the species has a slow metabolism and slow growth rate, and overfeeding rapidly degrades water quality.

goodWith: shell-dwellers (Lamprologus ocellatus, Neolamprologus multifasciatus, N. similis) — natural Lake Tanganyika biotope
other Lake Tanganyika rock-dwellers of similar or larger size (Julidochromis, Lepidiolamprologus attenuatus)
Lake Tanganyika goby cichlids (Eretmodus, Spathodus, Tanganicodus)
Synodontis petricola and other Lake Tanganyika catfish
avoidWith: small fish or fry that fit in its mouth — will be eaten
shell-dweller fry and eggs (compressiceps actively raids shell broods as natural predator)
soft-water or acid-water cichlids (Amazonian, West African)
fast-moving boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding time
Altolamprologus calvus in small tanks — conspecific territorial conflict
conspecific males in small tanks without visual barriers

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Altolamprologus compressiceps; adult specimen. Verify current URL on fishbase.se species page.
Wikimedia Commons image; confirm license and attribution on page before use.

summary: The Compressiceps (Altolamprologus compressiceps) is a Lake Tanganyika cichlid recognized for its strikingly flattened body, which it uses to probe rock crevices and raid shell-dweller nests. Several geographic color morphs are available in the trade. Maintaining hard, alkaline water is essential, and keepers should expect slow growth — juveniles may take years to reach maturity.
careNotes:
Altolamprologus compressiceps is one of the most laterally compressed cichlids in the hobby, its razor-thin body allowing it to wedge into the narrowest rock crevices and shell openings to hunt small fish, fry, and crustaceans in its native Lake Tanganyika. This extreme lateral compression directly enables the species' signature behavior: raiding the shell-broods of shell-dwelling Lamprologines.

Species confusion — naming clash: The name "Compressiceps" is used for two entirely unrelated fish. Altolamprologus compressiceps (this species) is a Lake Tanganyika cichlid; Dimidiochromis compressiceps is an unrelated Lake Malawi haplochromine predator sometimes also called "Compressiceps" or "Malawi Eye-Biter." The two species require completely different water conditions and are not interchangeable. Always verify the scientific name when purchasing.

Distinction from A. calvus: A. compressiceps is frequently confused with its sibling species A. calvus. The key field character is the forehead (dorsal head) profile viewed from the side — compressiceps has a straighter or gently rounded dorsal slope from the nape to the snout tip (the "rounded" profile), whereas calvus has a steeper, more concave ("hollow" or upturned) forehead. Both species share the same laterally compressed body, overlapping colour forms, and near-identical care requirements, but they are separate species with non-overlapping wild distributions within the lake. A. compressiceps also tends to reach slightly larger maximum sizes (males to ~15 cm) than calvus.

Geographic colour morphs: A. compressiceps is notable for the number of regionally distinct colour forms collected from different areas around the lake, all of which are valid collection localities sold under trade names. Common trade morphs include: Mutondwe Island (yellow/gold body — also called "Gold" or "Yellow"), Mwila Red Fin (orange-red fin markings), Gold Head (golden nuchal and head colouration), Pearl (pale silvery body with dark fin margins), Kigoma, Kapampa, and others. These are distinct wild collection localities; cross-breeding between morphs is discouraged by Tanganyikan cichlid specialists.

Growth rate: Among the slowest-growing cichlids in the hobby. Under ideal conditions, a juvenile at 3–4 cm may take 2–3 years to reach sexual maturity and a further 1–2 years to attain full adult size. Males reach ~15 cm; females remain smaller at ~8–10 cm. Avoid overfeeding to induce rapid growth — it can stress the fish and degrade water quality.

Water chemistry: Lake Tanganyika is among the world's most chemically stable lakes. Replicate this stability in captivity: target pH 8.2–9.0 with hard, well-buffered water (kH 8–18, gH 10–20). Use Tanganyika mineral buffers or crushed coral/aragonite substrate to maintain alkalinity. Prefer small, frequent water changes (10–15% weekly) over large infrequent ones to avoid parameter swings.

Community keeping: While the Compressiceps will readily take small fish or fry that fit in its mouth, it is generally not broadly aggressive toward unrelated tankmates of comparable or larger size. A classic Tanganyikan biotope pairs the Compressiceps with shell-dwellers — the natural predator-prey ecology mirrors the lake, with shell-dwellers generally at lower risk when provided ample shells, and the Compressiceps benefiting from the visual stimulus and the occasional predatory opportunity.

Feeding: Feed carnivore-appropriate frozen and prepared foods 2–3 times per week in modest portions. New specimens may refuse prepared foods — offer live or frozen brine shrimp and mysis to trigger the feeding response before transitioning to pellets.
breedingNotes: The Compressiceps is a substrate cave spawner. The female deposits eggs inside an empty snail shell (Neothauma or a suitable substitute) or within a narrow rock crevice small enough that the male cannot enter; her lateral compression allows her to guard the eggs within spaces inaccessible to most other fish. The female alone broods the eggs and newly hatched wrigglers while the male guards the broader surrounding territory. Clutch sizes are small relative to other cichlids, typically ranging from 20 to 100 eggs. Fry are tiny at hatching and require infusoria or commercial fry foods before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii; development is slow, with juveniles taking approximately 4–6 months to reach 2 cm. Breeding is considered advanced: stable, species-appropriate water chemistry is essential; the spawning site must be correctly sized; and pairing is not easily forced — the recommended approach is to raise a juvenile group of 4–6 fish and allow natural pair formation to occur. Separating the fry from the male once the female's brooding behavior breaks down tends to improve survival rates.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Altolamprologus compressiceps
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Lake Tanganyika rocky littoral), water parameters (pH 7.5–9.0, temperature 23–26°C, hardness 8–25 dH), adult size (males to 15 cm, females ~8 cm), diet (invertivore/piscivore), tank requirements, compatibility notes, breeding behaviour (shell/crevice spawner, female brooder), and species distinction from A. calvus (forehead profile). Notes geographic colour morphs as separate collection localities.

additional.name: FishBase — Altolamprologus compressiceps
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult TL (males to 15 cm), native range (Lake Tanganyika endemic — Burundi, DRC, Tanzania, Zambia), depth range (3–40 m), temperature (23–27°C), and lifespan estimates. Distribution map confirms restricted endemic range within Lake Tanganyika rocky littoral zone. Also confirmed separate species Dimidiochromis compressiceps (Lake Malawi) — a completely different genus causing the common-name clash.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Altolamprologus Cichlid Care
additional.notes: Trade-focused care reference for Altolamprologus genus. Confirmed geographic colour morph trade names (Mutondwe Yellow/Gold, Mwila Red Fin, Gold Head, Pearl), tank size recommendation (40 gal / 150 L for a pair, 55 gal / 208 L for community), slow growth rate warning, feeding notes (frozen carnivore foods, sparingly), and community pairing with shell-dwellers. Notes common name confusion with Lake Malawi Dimidiochromis compressiceps.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Altolamprologus compressiceps Profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source. Confirmed lateral compression as predatory adaptation for crevice and shell invasion, shell-dweller predator-prey community keeping notes, rounded dorsal head profile distinguishing compressiceps from calvus (steeper/concave), geographic colour form descriptions, and breeding notes (small clutch, slow fry development, female cave brooder). pH 8.2–9.0 and water stability requirements confirmed.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Altolamprologus: The Compressiceps Complex
additional.notes: TFH reference covering the Altolamprologus genus including calvus vs compressiceps identification. Confirmed rounded dorsal slope as primary identification character distinguishing A. compressiceps from A. calvus (steeper/concave), geographic colour forms as separate collection localities not to be interbred, lifespan estimates (8–12+ years), and advanced care designation. Also addressed common name confusion with Dimidiochromis compressiceps (Lake Malawi).

fish.breedingNotes: The female deposits eggs inside an empty snail shell or narrow rock crevice while the male guards the surrounding territory. Clutches are small, typically 20–100 eggs. Fry are tiny at hatching, require infusoria before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii, and develop slowly. Pair formation is challenging when starting with adults; raising a juvenile group of 4–6 and allowing natural pairing is the generally recommended approach. Water chemistry must remain stable and species-appropriate throughout spawning and rearing. The combination of demanding water parameters, the need for natural pair formation, and slow fry development places this species in the advanced breeding category.
